name: Yamaguchi criteria for Adult-Onset Still's Disease
citation: Yamaguchi M, et al. J Rheumatol. 1992;19(3):424-430.
classification_rule: At least 5 criteria (including at least 2 major) AND all exclusions absent.

major.label: Typical rash (evanescent, salmon-coloured, macular/maculopapular, usually on trunk/limbs during fever spikes)

exclusions.label: Other rheumatic disease (especially vasculitis, RA, SLE)

references.title: Preliminary criteria for classification of adult Still's disease
references.authors: Yamaguchi M, Ohta A, Tsunematsu T, et al.
references.why_it_matters: The 1992 Yamaguchi criteria remain the most widely used classification criteria for AOSD worldwide. Sensitivity ~96%, specificity ~92% in the derivation cohort.

references.title: Mechanisms, biomarkers and targets for adult-onset Still's disease
references.authors: Feist E, Mitrovic S, Fautrel B
references.why_it_matters: Current reference review on AOSD pathophysiology, biomarkers (ferritin, glycosylated ferritin, IL-18, IL-1β), and targeted therapy (anakinra, canakinumab, tocilizumab). Frames AOSD as autoinflammatory not autoimmune.

references.title: Adult-onset Still's disease
references.authors: Gerfaud-Valentin M, Jamilloux Y, Iwaz J, Sève P
references.why_it_matters: Comprehensive clinical review covering presentation patterns (monocyclic, polycyclic, chronic articular), complications (macrophage activation syndrome), and differential diagnosis vs mimics.

references.title: Diagnostic value of ferritin and glycosylated ferritin in adult onset Still's disease
references.authors: Fautrel B, Le Moël G, Saint-Marcoux B, et al.
references.why_it_matters: Glycosylated ferritin <20% (in a setting of very high total ferritin, often >5× ULN) is highly specific for AOSD. Useful when Yamaguchi criteria are borderline.

references.title: Life-threatening complications of adult-onset Still's disease
references.authors: Efthimiou P, Kadavath S, Mehta B
references.why_it_matters: Review of life-threatening AOSD complications including macrophage activation syndrome / secondary HLH (~12-15% of severe AOSD, ~10-22% mortality). Key flags: falling ferritin trajectory despite worsening, cytopenias, elevated triglycerides, low fibrinogen, hepatosplenomegaly.
